title: Energy security in a world shaped by supply | BlackRock
gist: BlackRock Investment Institute argues two geopolitical shocks in four years have revealed that fuel supply concentration in a few regions and chokepoints makes disruption a recurring systemic risk; AI, data centers, cooling, and EVs are accelerating structural electricity demand growth while domestic grid infrastructure creates new local bottlenecks—with near-term opportunities in energy security and long-term opportunities in electrification.

insights.claim: Two geopolitical shocks in four years have revealed that fuel supply remains concentrated in a handful of regions and strategic chokepoints, making disruption a recurring and system-wide risk—not a one-off event—that ripples through inflation, growth, and markets for the majority of the world's population living in net fuel-importing economies.
insights.speaker: BlackRock Investment Institute
insights.so_what: Energy exporters outside key chokepoints and infrastructure/power-system assets that deliver reliable domestic energy supply are benefiting structurally—BII explicitly highlights these as near-term investment winners.
insights.quote: disruption a recurring and systemwide risk, not a one-off event
insights.locator: Two geopolitical shocks in four years

insights.claim: Unlike globally traded fuels, electricity systems are local, real-time, and dependent on domestic infrastructure—as demand rises from AI, data centers, cooling, and EVs, bottlenecks in generation, transmission, storage, and grid infrastructure are becoming increasingly binding, raising the value of reliable and resilient power systems.
insights.speaker: BlackRock Investment Institute
insights.so_what: Domestic grid infrastructure, generation capacity adjacent to demand centers, and energy storage are the relevant investment categories—not global fuel supply chains—for the electricity security opportunity.
insights.locator: The rising value of electricity security

insights.claim: BII identifies two investment horizons: near-term—markets placing greater value on secure supply, flexibility, and resilience favor energy exporters outside chokepoints and infrastructure/power assets delivering reliable supply; long-term—repeated shocks and rising electricity demand strengthen the case for electrification, domestic power, grids, storage, and electrotech that reduces fuel dependence.
insights.speaker: BlackRock Investment Institute
insights.so_what: BII's two-horizon framework signals they are simultaneously holding near-term energy security plays (exporters, infrastructure) while building long-term electrification/decarbonization positions—investors should consider a similar barbell approach.
insights.locator: We see investment opportunities emerging across two horizons.

insights.claim: BII observes greater regional, sector, and technology dispersion as countries move at different speeds to manage energy security challenges—reinforcing the case for a selective and active investment approach rather than broad passive exposure to energy.
insights.speaker: BlackRock Investment Institute
insights.so_what: The energy transition is no longer a global convergence story—country-specific policy environments and infrastructure constraints are creating wide return dispersion, favoring active managers with energy sector expertise.
insights.quote: we see greater dispersion across regions, sectors and technologies — reinforcing the case for a selective and active investment approach
